title: Return Types
description: What a function's return type means, formalizing void, multiple return statements, and why code after a return never runs.
objectives: Understand what a function's return type declares
Understand that return immediately exits the function
Use multiple return statements for different paths

sections.text: Back in main() Function, we covered int main() returning an exit status. Every function has a return type, not just main() — it's the type of value the function promises to hand back to whoever calls it.
2. The Return Type Must Match What's Returned
A function's return type declares what kind of value the function will send back to its caller when it finishes.

double half(int n) { return n; } is technically allowed — n implicitly converts from int to double, as covered in Type Casting — but return "hello"; inside a function declared int would be a type mismatch and a compilation error.

void formally means "this function does not return a value." We previewed this in Function Calling — greet() is called purely for what it does (printing), not for a value to use. (This example also uses string, previewed here — we'll cover it properly soon.)
4. void Functions Can Still Use return
A void function can still write return; on its own — with no value after it — to exit the function early. It just can't write return someValue; since it has nothing to return.
The moment a return statement runs, the function ends immediately — no code after it, even in the same function, executes for that call. checkSign(5) returns 1 and never even looks at the second if.
7. Every Path Should Return (for a Non-void Function)

Recall from main() Function: only main() is allowed to implicitly return 0. Every other function that declares a non-void return type must actually return a value on every possible path — falling off the end without one leads to undefined behavior, and most compilers will warn about it.

sections.title: A vending machine that always dispenses something
sections.text: A vending machine that promises to return a snack for any valid button press had better actually dispense something for every button, not just some of them. A function's return type is that same promise — declare int, and every path through the function needs to deliver an int, no exceptions.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Declaring a non-void return type but leaving a path that doesn't return anything — undefined behavior, since the caller expects a value that was never produced.
Writing return someValue; inside a void function — a void function can only use a bare return; with no value.
Expecting code after a return statement (in the same block) to still run — it never does; return exits immediately.
Assuming a value like a string can be returned from an int function without any conversion — types must match or be convertible, as in Type Casting.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does a function's return type declare?
questions.answer: The type of value the function promises to send back to its caller when it finishes.

questions.question: What does void mean as a return type?
questions.answer: That the function does not return any value; it's called purely for its effect.

questions.question: Can a void function use the return keyword?
questions.answer: Yes — a void function can use a bare return; with no value, to exit the function early, but it cannot return an actual value.

questions.question: What happens immediately when a return statement executes?
questions.answer: The function ends immediately at that point — no further code in that function call executes, even if more statements follow in the same block.

questions.question: What's expected of every path through a non-void function?
questions.answer: Every possible path through the function should reach a return statement with a value of the declared return type — falling off the end without one leads to undefined behavior.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A function's return type declares what kind of value it promises to hand back to its caller — void means it returns nothing at all, and is called purely for its effect. A return statement exits the function immediately, so any code after it in that call never runs, and a non-void function needs a return with a matching value on every possible path through it, or the behavior is undefined. A void function can still use a bare return; to exit early, just without a value attached.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Return Types
sections.text: Return type = the promise. void = no promise made. return = keeps the promise and leaves immediately, no exceptions.
